question_text: Will there be more people with HIV/AIDS in 2037 than in 2017?

nodes.description: Level of financial resources allocated to HIV prevention and treatment programs
Proportion of HIV‑positive individuals receiving effective ART
Annual incidence of new HIV infections per 1,000 population
Death rate among people living with HIV/AIDS
Poverty, education, and employment levels influencing risk behaviors
Effectiveness of national HIV prevention, testing, and education policies
Overall population growth affecting absolute numbers of infected people
Social stigma and barriers that limit access to testing and treatment
More people living with HIV/AIDS in 2037 than in 2017

edges.mechanism: More funding expands ART programs, increasing coverage
Reduced stigma improves uptake of ART, raising coverage
Higher ART coverage lowers HIV‑related mortality
Poverty and limited education raise risky behaviors, increasing new infections
Strong policies promote prevention and testing, reducing new infections
Higher incidence adds to the total number of people living with HIV
Lower mortality means infected individuals remain alive longer, raising total count
A larger population raises the absolute number of infected individuals even if prevalence stays constant

reasoning: Global HIV prevalence peaked around 2005 and has been slowly declining due to expanding ART and prevention. Continued scale‑up of treatment and strong policies are likely to keep mortality low and new infections modest, while overall population growth adds some absolute cases. Given uncertainties in funding, socioeconomic trends, and stigma, there is a moderate chance (about 34%) that the total number of people living with HIV in 2037 will exceed the 2017 level.

probes.probe_text: Recent studies suggest that widespread drug resistance is rendering current ART regimens largely ineffective, so increasing coverage may not reduce HIV mortality at all. Moreover, the side‑effects and adherence challenges associated with long‑term ART could actually increase risky behaviors, potentially raising new infection rates rather than lowering them.
probes.description: Proportion of HIV‑positive individuals receiving effective ART

probes.probe_text: Recent advances in long‑acting ART and cure research suggest that mortality among people living with HIV will become negligible, so variations in HIV‑related death rates will have almost no impact on the total number of infected individuals by 2037. Moreover, even if mortality were to rise slightly, the constant influx of new infections and overall population growth would dominate the count, rendering mortality_rate_hiv largely irrelevant to the outcome.
probes.description: Death rate among people living with HIV/AIDS

probes.probe_text: Recent longitudinal studies in sub‑Saharan Africa have shown that HIV incidence remains stable across regions with vastly different poverty and education levels, suggesting that socioeconomic conditions may not drive risk behaviors as previously thought. Instead, network-level factors such as viral strain transmissibility and sexual partnership patterns appear to dominate new infection dynamics, rendering socioeconomic variables largely irrelevant for forecasting future case numbers.
probes.description: Poverty, education, and employment levels influencing risk behaviors

probes.probe_text: Recent studies in several high-prevalence regions have shown that, even where stigma is low and testing services are widely accepted, HIV incidence has not declined, suggesting that stigma may not be a primary driver of infection rates. Moreover, the observed improvements in treatment uptake appear to stem more from policy-driven outreach programs than from changes in social attitudes, calling into question the relevance of stigma_and_access_to_care as a causal factor in this model.
probes.description: Social stigma and barriers that limit access to testing and treatment

probes.probe_text: A 2025 WHO report shows that a newly implemented global funding initiative increased ART coverage from 62% to 78% of HIV‑positive individuals within two years, and a concurrent meta‑analysis found a 45% reduction in HIV‑related mortality in countries achieving >75% coverage. Moreover, Dr. Anita Patel, lead epidemiologist at the Global HIV Consortium, testified before the UN that expanding ART access is now the single most effective lever for lowering both prevalence and death rates, outweighing policy and socioeconomic interventions.
probes.description: Proportion of HIV‑positive individuals receiving effective ART

probes.probe_text: A 2025 WHO Global HIV Report revealed that, thanks to the rollout of next‑generation long‑acting ART regimens, the annual HIV‑related mortality rate fell by 58% worldwide between 2020 and 2024, with the most pronounced declines in low‑income regions. The report also cites an independent cohort study from 15 sub‑Saharan countries showing that improved survival has already increased the absolute number of people living with HIV by 12% over the past five years, despite stable incidence rates.
probes.description: Death rate among people living with HIV/AIDS

probes.probe_text: Recent data from the World Bank shows that between 2020 and 2025, unemployment rates in several high‑prevalence regions rose by 12 %, and a longitudinal study published in The Lancet found that communities experiencing this rise saw a 15 % increase in new HIV infections, independent of treatment coverage. The analysis attributes the surge to heightened transactional sex and reduced access to education, underscoring socioeconomic conditions as a key driver of HIV incidence.
probes.description: Poverty, education, and employment levels influencing risk behaviors

probes.probe_text: A recent multi‑country study published in The Lancet (2025) found that regions with high stigma scores experienced a 27% drop in HIV testing rates and a 15% lag in ART initiation compared to low‑stigma areas, despite similar funding levels. Moreover, a WHO report released this year identified stigma‑related barriers as responsible for 40% of missed treatment opportunities in sub‑Saharan Africa, directly linking heightened stigma to lower ART coverage and higher mortality.
probes.description: Social stigma and barriers that limit access to testing and treatment

probes.probe_text: Recent studies in sub‑Saharan Africa show that as ART coverage expands, the proportion of patients with multi‑drug resistant HIV has risen, leading to treatment failures and mortality rates that no longer decline proportionally. Moreover, limited healthcare infrastructure can cause poor adherence and opportunistic infections, weakening the assumed mortality benefit.
probes.description: Higher ART coverage lowers HIV‑related mortality

probes.probe_text: Recent studies show that as HIV mortality declines, many infected individuals experience long-term health complications that reduce fertility and increase migration out of high‑prevalence regions, which can actually lower the number of people living with HIV by 2037. Moreover, lower mortality may shift health resources toward chronic care, limiting funding for new prevention efforts and thus offsetting any gains in prevalence.
probes.description: Lower mortality means infected individuals remain alive longer, raising total count

probes.probe_text: Even if the overall population grows, the increase may be concentrated in age groups or regions with very low HIV risk, so the absolute number of infected people could stay flat or even drop despite constant prevalence in the high‑risk subpopulation. Moreover, rapid demographic expansion can strain health systems, prompting intensified prevention efforts that reduce prevalence faster than the population grows, breaking the assumed direct link.
probes.description: A larger population raises the absolute number of infected individuals even if prevalence stays constant

probes.probe_text: A 2023 WHO Global HIV Report found that countries achieving >95% antiretroviral coverage saw a 70% drop in HIV‑related mortality within two years, largely driven by the recent rollout of long‑acting injectable ART that dramatically improves adherence. This rapid mortality decline suggests the protective effect of ART coverage is substantially stronger than previously estimated.
probes.description: Higher ART coverage lowers HIV‑related mortality

probes.probe_text: A 2025 WHO report found that global HIV‑related mortality dropped by 45% due to expanded ART, yet the absolute number of people living with HIV rose to a record 40.5 million, showing that reduced mortality directly inflates prevalence. Additionally, a longitudinal cohort study in sub‑Saharan Africa reported that a 10% decline in mortality led to a 7% increase in the total HIV‑positive population over ten years, underscoring the strength of this link.
probes.description: Lower mortality means infected individuals remain alive longer, raising total count

probes.probe_text: Recent data from several low‑income countries show that spikes in HIV‑related mortality often precede drops in reported antiretroviral coverage, because when mortality rises sharply, many patients die before they can be enrolled in treatment programs, causing coverage rates to fall. Could it be that higher mortality actually drives lower observed ART coverage rather than the other way around?
probes.description: Higher ART coverage lowers HIV‑related mortality

probes.probe_text: Recent demographic studies show that rapid population growth in high‑risk regions expands the pool of sexually active individuals, directly increasing the absolute number of new HIV infections even when per‑thousand incidence stays constant. Ignoring the pathway from demographic_growth to new_infection_rate therefore underestimates how population expansion can drive higher infection counts, making this edge crucial for accurate forecasting.
probes.description: Potential link: Overall population growth affecting absolute numbers of infected people -> Annual incidence of new HIV infections per 1,000 population

probes.probe_text: Recent studies indicate that rapid population growth can strain healthcare resources, potentially reducing the per‑patient availability of HIV treatment and increasing HIV‑related mortality. Could the rise in overall population dilute funding and healthcare capacity, thereby raising the mortality_rate_hiv? Please consider adding a direct link from demographic_growth to mortality_rate_hiv.
probes.description: Potential link: Overall population growth affecting absolute numbers of infected people -> Death rate among people living with HIV/AIDS

probes.probe_text: A major factor omitted is the scale of pre‑exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) availability and uptake. Wider PrEP coverage directly lowers the new_infection_rate by preventing transmission among high‑risk groups, which would in turn reduce the number of people living with HIV in 2037.
probes.description: A plausible factor not in the current network

probes.probe_text: Recent studies show that the emergence of antiretroviral drug resistance significantly reduces treatment effectiveness, leading to higher mortality and increased viral load among treated patients. Including a node for 'antiretroviral_resistance_prevalence'—which influences both mortality_rate_hiv and new_infection_rate—could change the forecast for more_hiv_people_2037.
probes.description: Another plausible missing factor

probes.probe_text: Recent surveys show that a popular streaming series released in 2025 featured a main character who is HIV‑positive, leading to increased online discussions about the disease. The series has sparked a wave of fan art and memes but does not change funding levels, treatment coverage, infection rates, mortality, socioeconomic conditions, policy strength, demographic growth, or stigma in any measurable way.
probes.description: Topically related but causally irrelevant information

probes.probe_text: A recent fashion magazine featured a spread showcasing clothing lines inspired by the colors of the HIV awareness ribbon. The article highlighted how designers are using these colors to promote solidarity, but it does not discuss any changes in funding, treatment, or infection rates.
probes.description: Another topically related but irrelevant piece of information

probe_results.probe_text: Recent studies suggest that widespread drug resistance is rendering current ART regimens largely ineffective, so increasing coverage may not reduce HIV mortality at all. Moreover, the side‑effects and adherence challenges associated with long‑term ART could actually increase risky behaviors, potentially raising new infection rates rather than lowering them.
probe_results.reasoning: The new evidence undermines two key protective links in the causal model. If ART coverage no longer lowers HIV‑related mortality, the mortality_rate_hiv node will not decrease with higher coverage, removing a major brake on the total number of people living with HIV. Moreover, if long‑term ART use raises risky behaviors, the antiretroviral_coverage node may actually increase new_infection_rate rather than suppress it. Both changes shift the net effect toward a larger HIV population in 2037, raising the probability above the original 0.34. The other nodes (funding, policy, socioeconomic conditions, demographic growth) remain unchanged, so the overall forecast moves upward but not to certainty, leading to an updated probability of about 0.58.

If ART coverage no longer lowers HIV‑related mortality, the mortality_rate_hiv node will not decrease with higher coverage, removing a major brake on the total number of people living with HIV. Moreover, if long‑term ART use raises risky behaviors, the antiretroviral_coverage node may actually increase new_infection_rate rather than suppress it. Both changes shift the net effect toward a larger HIV population in 2037, raising the probability above the original 0.34. The other nodes (funding, policy, socioeconomic conditions, demographic growth) remain unchanged, so the overall forecast moves upward but not to certainty, leading to an updated probability of about 0.58."}

probe_results.probe_text: Recent advances in long‑acting ART and cure research suggest that mortality among people living with HIV will become negligible, so variations in HIV‑related death rates will have almost no impact on the total number of infected individuals by 2037. Moreover, even if mortality were to rise slightly, the constant influx of new infections and overall population growth would dominate the count, rendering mortality_rate_hiv largely irrelevant to the outcome.
probe_results.reasoning: The new information weakens the causal link from mortality_rate_hiv to the outcome. With HIV‑related mortality assumed negligible, the increase in total cases will depend mainly on new infections and demographic growth. Global trends indicate declining incidence and only moderate population growth, reducing the likelihood that the absolute number of people living with HIV in 2037 will exceed the 2017 level. Hence the probability is revised downward from 0.34 to about 0.25.

Hence the probability is revised downward from 0.34 to about 0.25."}

probe_results.probe_text: Recent longitudinal studies in sub‑Saharan Africa have shown that HIV incidence remains stable across regions with vastly different poverty and education levels, suggesting that socioeconomic conditions may not drive risk behaviors as previously thought. Instead, network-level factors such as viral strain transmissibility and sexual partnership patterns appear to dominate new infection dynamics, rendering socioeconomic variables largely irrelevant for forecasting future case numbers.
probe_results.reasoning: The new evidence weakens the causal link from socioeconomic_conditions to new_infection_rate. Since poverty and education were previously considered contributors to higher incidence, removing that influence reduces the expected growth in new infections. The remaining drivers (funding, ART coverage, mortality, demographic growth, policy strength) are either neutral or point toward lower prevalence (e.g., higher ART reduces mortality and may reduce transmission). Consequently the chance that the absolute number of people living with HIV in 2037 exceeds that in 2017 is lower than the original 0.34 estimate.

probe_results.probe_text: Recent studies in several high-prevalence regions have shown that, even where stigma is low and testing services are widely accepted, HIV incidence has not declined, suggesting that stigma may not be a primary driver of infection rates. Moreover, the observed improvements in treatment uptake appear to stem more from policy-driven outreach programs than from changes in social attitudes, calling into question the relevance of stigma_and_access_to_care as a causal factor in this model.
probe_results.reasoning: The new evidence weakens the assumed causal link from stigma_and_access_to_care to antiretroviral_coverage and new_infection_rate. If stigma reduction does not substantially lower incidence, the expected decline in new infections is smaller than previously thought. Meanwhile, policy‑driven outreach still boosts treatment uptake, lowering mortality and keeping infected individuals alive longer. The net effect is a higher likelihood that the total number of people living with HIV in 2037 will exceed the 2017 count, so the probability is revised upward.

Moreover, Dr. Anita Patel, lead epidemiologist at the Global HIV Consortium, testified before the UN that expanding ART access is now the single most effective lever for lowering both prevalence and death rates, outweighing policy and socioeconomic interventions.
probe_results.reasoning: The WHO report shows ART coverage rising from 62% to 78% and a 45% drop in HIV‑related mortality where coverage exceeds 75%. The expert testimony confirms that high ART coverage now outweighs other levers in reducing both new infections and deaths. Higher treatment uptake cuts the new_infection_rate (through viral suppression) and lowers mortality_rate_hiv, both of which directly reduce the total number of people living with HIV in 2037. Since the causal network assigns strong influence to antiretroviral_coverage on the outcome, the new evidence shifts the forecast toward a lower chance that the 2037 count will exceed the 2017 count, moving the probability down from 0.34 to about 0.20.

The report also cites an independent cohort study from 15 sub‑Saharan countries showing that improved survival has already increased the absolute number of people living with HIV by 12% over the past five years, despite stable incidence rates.
probe_results.reasoning: The new WHO report shows a 58% drop in HIV‑related mortality from 2020‑2024 and a 12% rise in the absolute number of people living with HIV despite stable incidence. In the causal network, a lower mortality_rate_hiv directly reduces deaths among existing cases, raising the total population of people with HIV (mortality_rate_hiv -> more_hiv_people_2037). The observed 12% increase over five years demonstrates this effect in practice, implying that the mortality reduction will continue to contribute to a larger HIV‑positive population by 2037. Combined with ongoing demographic growth and unchanged new_infection_rate, the likelihood that more people will be living with HIV in 2037 than in 2017 rises substantially from the original 0.34 estimate.

probe_results.reasoning: The new World Bank and Lancet evidence directly strengthens the causal link from worsening socioeconomic_conditions to a higher new_infection_rate. A 12% rise in unemployment produced a 15% increase in HIV incidence independent of treatment coverage, implying that the baseline incidence will be substantially higher than previously assumed. Higher incidence adds more individuals to the HIV-positive pool, and even with ongoing ART expansion, the net effect is an increased likelihood that the absolute number of people living with HIV in 2037 will exceed the 2017 total. Consequently, the probability estimate rises from 0.34 to roughly 0.48.

probe_results.probe_text: Recent data from several low‑income countries show that spikes in HIV‑related mortality often precede drops in reported antiretroviral coverage, because when mortality rises sharply, many patients die before they can be enrolled in treatment programs, causing coverage rates to fall.
